non ces production functions i know ces production functions dominate economics, but i was curious, why? i've never seen a research paper or presentation utilize any form of a production function that is not ces.  why is imposing ces so important in our models? non ces production functions i know ces production functions dominate economics, but i was curious, why? i've never seen a research paper or presentation utilize any form of a production function that is not ces.  why is imposing ces so important in our models? the constant elasticity of substitution (ces) production function is widely used in economics due to its mathematical convenience, flexibility, and consistency with empirical observations about substitutability between inputs. the ces form allows for a straightforward and tractable way to model how easily one input (like labor) can be substituted for another input (like capital), thanks to the parameter that directly controls the elasticity of substitution. this flexibility encompasses several other commonly-used production functions: the cobb-douglas (a special case of ces with an elasticity of substitution of one) and the leontief (another special case with zero substitutability).

researchers and policymakers often choose ces non ces production functions i know ces production functions dominate economics, but i was curious, why? i've never seen a research paper or presentation utilize any form of a production function that is not ces. why is imposing ces so important in our models?

the variable \( d_i \) is considered the moderator for the relationship between \( p_{i,t} \) (the independent variable) and \( y_{ interaction terms enable you to examine whether the relationship between the target and a feature changes depending on the value of another feature. by adding these new variables to the regression model, you can measure the effects of the interaction between them and the target. it is crucial to interpret the coefficients of the interaction terms carefully to understand the direction and the strength of the relationship. an important, and often forgotten, concept in regression analysis is that of interaction terms.

did capitalism and adam smith support a central bank?  does adam smith and capitalism support a central bank/federal reserve?  i am trying to read through the book "wealth of nations" to understand. having a central/regulatory figure dictate supply and interest rates, seems less laissez faire. however curious if adam smith has any documentation on this subject. did capitalism and adam smith support a central bank?  does adam smith and capitalism support a central bank/federal reserve?  i am trying to read through the book "wealth of nations" to understand. having a central/regulatory figure dictate supply and interest rates, seems less laissez faire. however curious if adam smith has any documentation on this subject. adam smith, often regarded as the father of modern economics, wrote "the wealth of nations" in 1776, long before the concepts of central banking or the federal reserve as we know them today existed. his economic philosophy is rooted in the idea of the "invisible hand," advocating for free markets with limited government intervention—a principle commonly associated with laissez-faire capitalism.

in "the wealth of nations," smith supported the notion of free competition in banking and was skeptical of monopolies and exclusive privileges granted by the government, including central banks. he discussed the dangers of allowing a single bank, such as the bank of england in his time, wealth of nations state capitalism,

is it always a trade off between efficiency and equity?  is there any situations where we can achieve both equity and efficiency? i'm thinking of covid 19 vaccine program which is run by goverment. although the cost for the program is paid by the money from taxes, it still helps with economic growth right? (in my mind, it's like if people are saved they will have a normal life and buy stuffs, which will boost the production) or is there anything wrong in my mind? is it always a trade off between efficiency and equity?  is there any situations where we can achieve both equity and efficiency? i'm thinking of covid 19 vaccine program which is run by goverment. although the cost for the program is paid by the money from taxes, it still helps with economic growth right? (in my mind, it's like if people are saved they will have a normal life and buy stuffs, which will boost the production) or is there anything wrong in my mind? the relationship between efficiency and equity is often described as a trade-off: policies that promote equity (fairness in the distribution of resources) might reduce efficiency (maximizing total output or welfare), and vice versa. however, this trade-off is not absolute. there are situations where both goals can be achieved simultaneously, especially when policies designed to increase equity also contribute positively to overall economic performance.

the covid-19 vaccine program is a good example. by providing free or subsidized vaccines to all citizens, regardless of income, the government improves equity, ensuring that everyone has equal access to life-saving healthcare regardless of their ability to pay. at the safe and effective vaccines are a key tool to control the pandemic; however, vaccination programs can be costly. are the benefits they bestow worth the costs they incur?

"  from this link, the definition of social science is: "social science is, in its broadest sense, the study of society and the manner in which people behave and influence the world around us."  based on this definition, why economics relating to social science, and what is other than social science what is social science and discussion about type ii error preference?  today, my senior lecturer in economics and finance class teaching about laws impact companies' operation told us one sentence "also keep in mind that in (at least social) science, we care (try to avoid) about type ii (making a claim on some new finding while it is actually not true), more than type i error (rejecting some new findings while it is actually true). "  from this link, the definition of social science is: "social science is, in its broadest sense, the study of society and the manner in which people behave and influence the world around us."  based on this definition, why economics relating to social science, and what is other than social science social science, as defined, is the study of society and how people behave and impact the world around them. this broad field includes disciplines that analyze human relationships, institutions, and structures—such as sociology, psychology, political science, anthropology, and, importantly, economics. economics is considered a social science because it seeks to understand how individuals and groups make choices about resource allocation, production, and consumption, and how these decisions affect societies as a whole. economic theories and models aim to explain real-world outcomes, often considering factors like incentives, markets, and government policies, all within the framework of human behavior and societal impact.

in the context of the subject matter of economics places it clearly as a social science, but many in the discipline act as if it were a science. economics has gradually developed into an interdisciplinary subject that uses scientific methods, for example setting out and formally testing hypotheses, and particularly a mathematical approach, to solve social science problems. it is a social science pursued with more quantitative rigour than much of the rest of the social sciences.

have the deregulation measures that caused the 2007-2008 crisis been rolled back?  the 2007-2008 financial crisis was largely attributed to several deregulation measures, especially the gramm-leach-bliley act. have any of these measures been rolled back after the crisis, or new ones introduced to prevent a similar crisis? have the deregulation measures that caused the 2007-2008 crisis been rolled back?  the 2007-2008 financial crisis was largely attributed to several deregulation measures, especially the gramm-leach-bliley act. have any of these measures been rolled back after the crisis, or new ones introduced to prevent a similar crisis? after the 2007-2008 financial crisis, which was partly attributed to deregulation—especially the gramm-leach-bliley act of 1999 that repealed parts of the glass-steagall act and allowed commercial and investment banking to merge—there were significant legislative efforts to address the weaknesses in the financial regulatory system exposed by the crisis. however, most of the actual deregulation measures that enabled risky banking practices have not been fully rolled back.

instead of reinstating strict separation between commercial and investment banking, policymakers responded by introducing new regulations aimed at reducing systemic risk and increasing oversight. the most notable of these measures was the dodd the one deregulation law that is constantly discussed and associated with the crisis is the gramm-leach-bliley act because it repealed regulations that were put in place by the glass-steagall act a few years following the great depression. financial crises are not completely avoidable, but knowing what caused the 2008 financial crisis will help to change banking legislation from being reactive to proactive.

bootstrap always valid under asymptotic normality?  if an estimator is known to have an asymptotically normal distribution, is that sufficient for the bootstrap to be valid?  it seems that is must be, but in 20 minutes of googling i have come up empty on a proof. bootstrap always valid under asymptotic normality?  if an estimator is known to have an asymptotically normal distribution, is that sufficient for the bootstrap to be valid?  it seems that is must be, but in 20 minutes of googling i have come up empty on a proof. the fact that an estimator has an asymptotically normal distribution is *not* sufficient by itself to guarantee that the bootstrap is valid. while asymptotic normality is a key ingredient for many inferential procedures, the validity of the bootstrap depends on more than just the limiting distribution.

the bootstrap works by resampling the observed data to approximate the sampling distribution of a statistic. for it to be valid (i.e., for the distribution of the bootstrap estimator to converge in probability to the true sampling distribution), certain regularity conditions must be met. asymptotic normality means that, as the sample size "beran and ducharme (1991) give general conditions under which the bootstrap is consistent

has monopoly theory incorporated network effects as a source of monopoly?  i studied industrial organization for my econ. ph.d. four decades ago. at that time industrial organization had no way to incorporate 'network effects' into monopoly theory. with the advent of social media (especially facebook) since then accusations of monopoly power against social media are heard daily. has someone articulated a theory of network effects as the source of monopoly?  many thanks. has monopoly theory incorporated network effects as a source of monopoly?  i studied industrial organization for my econ. ph.d. four decades ago. at that time industrial organization had no way to incorporate 'network effects' into monopoly theory. with the advent of social media (especially facebook) since then accusations of monopoly power against social media are heard daily. has someone articulated a theory of network effects as the source of monopoly?  many thanks. yes, contemporary monopoly theory has substantially incorporated network effects as a source of monopoly power—an important evolution since the era in which you studied industrial organization. traditionally, monopoly theory emphasized cost structures such as economies of scale and barriers to entry arising from control of essential facilities or government regulation. however, beginning in the 1980s and especially accelerating with the rise of digital platforms, economists began to rigorously analyze network effects—situations in which the value of a good or service increases with the number of users—as a distinct and powerful barrier to entry and a source of market dominance.

network effects are now recognized as a key driver behind the emergence switching costs and network effects bind customers to vendors if products are incompatible, locking customers or even markets in to early choices.

us population growth: "natural increase" vs "fertility rate"  based on the 2017 us census population projections through 2060, specifically table 1 (projected population size and births, deaths, and migration), the "natural increase," which is us births - us deaths, was positive in 2017 at over 1 million more births than deaths, and was projected to remain positive through 2060, reaching a minimum of 400,000 in 2050 before beginning to increase again.  on the other hand, the fertility rate per woman for the us was 1.8 in 2017, according to the world bank. this is well below the replacement rate of 2.1.  how is it possible that the us could have over 1 million more births than deaths, and that a positive natural increase would be projected to continue for 40 years, but be well below replacement fertility rate?  this question is related, but the answers there indicate that it would be temporary situation due to population momentum. however, here the census bureau is predicting it will continue for 40 years. us population growth: "natural increase" vs "fertility rate"  based on the 2017 us census population projections through 2060, specifically table 1 (projected population size and births, deaths, and migration), the "natural increase," which is us births - us deaths, was positive in 2017 at over 1 million more births than deaths, and was projected to remain positive through 2060, reaching a minimum of 400,000 in 2050 before beginning to increase again.  on the other hand, the fertility rate per woman for the us was 1.8 in 2017, according to the world bank. this is well below the replacement rate of 2.1.  how is it possible that the us could have over 1 million more births than deaths, and that a positive natural increase would be projected to continue for 40 years, but be well below replacement fertility rate?  this question is related, but the answers there indicate that it would be temporary situation due to population momentum. however, here the census bureau is predicting it will continue for 40 years. certainly! here’s a clear passage answering the question:

the seemingly contradictory situation in which the us maintains a positive "natural increase" (more births than deaths) for several decades—even as its fertility rate remains well below the replacement level of 2.1 children per woman—can be explained by the concept of "population momentum." 

population momentum refers to the lagged impact of past high fertility rates on current population growth. although the fertility rate in the us was only 1.8 in 2017 (below replacement), the large number of people who are currently of childbearing age—thanks to higher birth rates in prior decades— **population.** in cbo’s projections, the social security area population ... increases from 342 million people in 2024 to 383 million people in 2054. ... as growth of the population age 65 or older outpaces growth of younger age groups, the population continues to become older, on average.

a specific economic term that captures the disincentive for "doing something first," particularly when imitators can reap similar rewards without bearing the initial costs or risks, is the "free rider problem." in this scenario, the first group incurs the expense and uncertainty of innovation, while subsequent groups can more easily replicate the success and enjoy comparable benefits. unlike the tragedy of the commons—which typically describes overuse and depletion of a resource—the free rider problem centers on under-provision of a good or activity because individuals or groups anticipate that others will benefit from their contribution without reciprocating. when the most sharks are cold-blooded. some, like the mako and the great white shark, are partially warm-blooded (they are endotherms). actually, the salmon shark is a warm-blooded shark. great white sharks are some of the only warm-blooded sharks.

re-indexing key performance indicators (kpis) such as the consumer price index (cpi) is a standard practice in economic statistics. organizations like eurostat periodically "rebase" the index, meaning they change the reference year (the year when the index is set to 100). for example, you observed that eurostat's cpi uses reference years 1996, 2005, and 2015.

the main reason for re-indexing is to keep the index relevant and accurate over time. economic structures, consumption patterns, and the importance of various goods and services change as society the internationally agreed reference year for the hicp is 1996. the harmonised consumer price index (hicp) has been specially developed as a comparable measure of inflation for all member states of the european union (eu). both the cpi and the hicp are based on the spending pattern of households in 2000. the internationally agreed reference year for the hicp is 1996.

log transformation of zeros (slave trade data)  i am trying to log transform data on slave trade voyages from the database by david eltis,the trans-atlantic slave trade database.  the problem i am facing: many values are 0. my first idea was to assign value 1 to all values which are 0, so after log transforming they are 0. however, since i am taking average voyages per year, for many years the value is < 1, leading to negative values after the log transformation.  so now i have smaller values for years in which there was slave trade (but <1) compared to years when there was no slave trade.  how does one adress such a problem? log transformation of zeros (slave trade data)  i am trying to log transform data on slave trade voyages from the database by david eltis,the trans-atlantic slave trade database.  the problem i am facing: many values are 0. my first idea was to assign value 1 to all values which are 0, so after log transforming they are 0. however, since i am taking average voyages per year, for many years the value is < 1, leading to negative values after the log transformation.  so now i have smaller values for years in which there was slave trade (but <1) compared to years when there was no slave trade.  how does one adress such a problem? when working with historical data such as the trans-atlantic slave trade database, zero values are common, especially when aggregating data to yearly levels where some years saw no voyages. log-transforming such data presents a challenge because the logarithm of zero is undefined. one common approach is to add a small constant to all values before applying the log transformation—for example, log(x + c)—where c is typically 1 or a value smaller than your smallest meaningful data point.

however, as you've noticed, if you are analyzing average voyages per year, some averages may be less than 1. with log transformation, these values become negative, so one problem with taking the logarithm of a variable is that it does not allow retaining zero-valued observations because ln(0) is undefined. but economic data often include meaningful zero-valued observations, and applied econometricians are typically loath to drop those observations for which the logarithm is undefined.

i suppose that even though one cannot have a "run" on a central bank, it could be seen as a bop or fx crisis. negative accounting equity for a central bank becomes a macroprudential concern primarily when it undermines confidence in the central bank’s capacity to pursue its policy objectives—especially price stability—or when it signals deteriorating sovereign or macroeconomic fundamentals. unlike commercial banks, central banks are not subject to runs by depositors because their liabilities (currency in circulation) are not redeemable for other assets on demand, and they have the unique capacity to create money. as such, technical insolvency—where liabilities exceed assets on a mark-to-market basis—does not directly impede their ability to operate in the short run.

however, negative central bank equity can register on first, all central banks have emphasized that there will be no impediments to their ability to conduct monetary policy, even if they temporarily make losses or have negative equity.

would the more robust global economy resulting from the abolition of trade be worth any costs? what would happen to the economy?  how can a giant volcanic eruption devastate the world? aside from the immediate fatalities, the eruption can devastate the world’s economy and climate cannot be understated. a good comparison would be the last recorded magnitude 7 eruption in tambora. while the death toll was severe, it impacted the entire world. the eruption launched huge amounts of volcanic ash, water and sulphuric acid into the atmosphere, obscuring the sun and repelling some solar radiation. the tambora eruption’s volcanic winter hit hard in 1816, which has become known as the year without a summer. temperatures dropped worldwide and climate and weather changes were felt everywhere. but europe and north america were arguably the most severely hit, with lakes and rivers being frozen over in july and august. crop damage was rampant, harvests ruined and food shortages widespread. food prices skyrocketed and violent riots erupted. malnourishment soon became a severe issue, facilitating disease epidemics that killed tens of thousands. could it get worse? the world is already experiencing severe food shortages and rising prices, though these were largely caused by inflation and the russo-ukraine war. however, incidents like this, the covid-19 pandemic and the blocking of the suez canal serve to underscore just how fragile the global supply chain is.  given these risks, would it be better for the economy to abolish trade completely and develop a principle of self-sufficiency, where a state will attempt to find any way to make and obtain its resources and products completely by itself if it is even remotely possible to do so? the argument that the fed’s open market operations (omos) do not affect the money supply contains some misunderstandings about how monetary policy and financial assets interact. while it is true that omos involve exchanging one kind of highly liquid asset (central bank reserves or cash) for another (treasury securities), this overlooks important institutional realities and standard definitions of the money supply.

first, central bank reserves—created when the fed purchases government bonds from the financial system—are fundamentally different from treasury securities in terms of their role in the banking system. reserves are the balances that commercial banks hold at the federal reserve and are the foundation in no way does the aggregate quantity of reserves directly constrain the amount of bank lending or deposit creation. bank deposits are simply a record of how much the bank itself owes its customers. so they are a liability of the bank, not an asset that could be lent out. a related misconception is that banks can lend out their reserves. reserves can only be lent between banks, since consumers do not have access to reserves accounts at the bank of england.

low nominal interest rates generally encourage lending rather than discourage it, especially within the context of expansionary monetary policy. when the federal reserve (the fed) adopts expansionary policies—such as purchasing government bonds—it increases the money supply and decreases interest rates. the primary goal of this policy is to make borrowing cheaper for businesses and consumers. lower interest rates reduce the cost of obtaining loans, which encourages borrowing and investment in the economy. firms are more likely to invest in new projects, and individuals are more likely to buy homes or cars when loan payments are more affordable.

is there a long-run tradeoff between inflation and unemployment?  in most first-year undergrad macroeconomics courses, students are taught about the phillips curve and how whilst there may exist a tradeoff between inflation and unemployment in the short-run, there is no such thing in the long-run as a result of agents adjusting inflation expectations. is this result valid or could it be the case that the natural rate of unemployment changes with the output gap, in which case there could exist a long-run trade-off between inflation and unemployment? in most introductory macroeconomics courses, students learn about the phillips curve, which depicts a negative relationship between inflation and unemployment in the short run. this implies that policymakers might be able to reduce unemployment temporarily by allowing higher inflation. however, this relationship is believed to break down in the long run, as articulated by economists such as milton friedman and edmund phelps. according to their analysis, over time, people adjust their expectations of inflation. as expectations catch up to actual inflation, the short-run phillips curve shifts upwards, and the economy returns to its "natural rate of unemployment" (often denoted as the non-accelerating inflation rate of the long-run phillips curve (henceforth, lrpc)—describing the alternative combinations of inflation and the unemployment rate that an economy can achieve in equilibrium—plays a central role in monetary policy. if the lrpc is vertical there is no long-run trade-off between the two variables, and the central bank ought therefore simply aim at keeping inflation low and stable.

the basic relationship is:

lower interest rates make borrowing cheaper. this encourages people and businesses to take out more loans and spend more money. more spending means more money moving around in the economy, which tends to push prices up—this is higher inflation. in short:  
lower interest rates → more borrowing and spending → higher inflation.

 update: adding a great explaination of "pluralistic ignorance" as suggested by @lason  the term “pluralistic ignorance” was coined to describe the situation in which almost all members of a group privately reject group norms, yet believe that virtually all other group members accept them (katz and allport, 1931). under such situations, individuals predict that they would lose social standing if they behaved as they wished. behaving against the group norm could result in negative reactions from other ingroup members. therefore, people are likely to follow perceived group norms to maintain positive impressions in their groups, even when they do not support the norms (miller and mcfarland, 1987; miller and prentice, 1994; prentice and miller, 1996; geiger and swim, 2016). in line with this idea, in situations of pluralistic ignorance, some people even actively enforce the perceived norms (i.e., publicly criticizing a “misfit” into accepting the norm), although they privately disapprove of the norms (willer et al., 2009). consequently, public behaviors of groups as a whole do not coincide with the majority of group members' private preferences under circumstances of pluralistic ignorance. thus, the situation of pluralistic ignorance is well represented in the following sentence: “no one believes, but everyone believes that everyone else believes” (krech and crutchfield, 1948). is there any example for "pluralistic ignorance" in economics? was there any currency backed by other metals than gold, silver or copper?  was any other metal than gold, silver or copper ever in history used to back a currency? was there any currency backed by other metals than gold, silver or copper?  was any other metal than gold, silver or copper ever in history used to back a currency? yes, throughout history, a few other metals besides gold, silver, and copper have been used to back or constitute currency, although such instances are relatively rare. one notable example is iron money, which was used in some parts of ancient china, particularly during the zhou and han dynasties. large iron coins and even tools made of iron were sometimes used as currency, especially in regions where precious metals were scarce.

another example is tin currency, as seen on the indonesian island of bangka and in the malay archipelago, where tin "hat" money and tin ingots were used for trade and as a medium of exchange. lead coins hard metals
